data.title: Texas Pacific Land Corporation: Back To Normal
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Today, I want to express my opinion on Texas Pacific Land Corporation (NYSE:TPL), whose stock has a 6-month return of -32%. After careful quantitative research, I have come to the conclusion that the current market environment presents an exceptional opportunity to buy a remarkable stock at an outstanding price. Therefore, I am assigning TPL a buy rating based on my interpretation of key financials and price action.

Thesis Disclaimer

I use a buy rating, but my approach would be better served by a rating scale from avoid to attractive. Attractive status or buy rating relative to TPL stock is based on an analysis of the financial performance of the business, taking into account an assessment of the current value. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3-5 year horizon, and it does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

Asset-Light Royalty Model Makes Valuation Premium

With 873,000 acres in Texas (primarily in the Permian Basin), the company is a prime example of a high-margin rentier business. TPL does not produce or process oil or gas, and ~57% of revenue is generated from oil and gas royalties as of Q1 2025. The Permian Basin is one of the largest oil and gas regions in the US and globally, and oil and gas production in this region requires huge volumes of water for fracking, drilling, and well cooling. In total, the company sold water and the right to receive it for $66.5m in Q1, which is another 34% of revenue. Easements and other surface-related income account for the remaining 9% of revenue, and none of these items involve production costs or require high operating expenses.

As a result, we see the highest levels of gross margin (93%), EBIT margin (77%), and net profit margin (63%). Usually, I interpret such indicators as the presence of an undeniable competitive advantage, but in this story, there are no competitors as such. And the advantage is the business model itself and the possession of land in such a sought-after region.

The secondary advantage of this model is the absence of a debt burden. Apart from the minimum capital lease volumes, TPL is a strong debt-free business, aimed at accumulating cash flow and returning it to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

Free Cash Flow Sharp Drop

However, in 2024, the stability of this paradigm was disrupted. Free cash flow decreased 5 times relative to the 5-year average, and this greatly frightened the investment community.

In 2024, the company paid a special additional dividend of $10 per share. The yield on this payment was negligible, but it spurred interest in the stock and initiated the subsequent explosive price growth (which almost tripled by the end of 2024).

Not only because of the dividend, but there were other reasons for this growth. Record production volumes and rising oil prices put the expected royalties under positive revision. Rapid growth in the water segment added confidence in the future, and active share buybacks and their entry into the S&P 500 Index was a pleasant bonus. The management signal and the strategy of readiness to share profits with shareholders became the beginning of a large but short-term bubble.

However, at the end of 2024, the company suddenly changed its own policy, and in the second half of the year, almost $400m was spent on the purchase of intangible assets—a significant amount for a business.

The company has suspended buybacks until at least the cash pile exceeds $700m, and the special dividend payment is unlikely to be repeated in the near future.

Сash Flow Fell To Grow

To be honest, I'm rather trying to somehow justify the volatility we've witnessed in 2024. But I don't see any objective fundamental reasons for either hyper growth or the subsequent 50% correction.

TPL invested in acquiring new acres, contractual rights to royalties, and significant mineral and royalty interest in the Midland Basin, adding almost 7.5 thousand to its net royalty acres, increasing the overall portfolio by almost 40%!

Having somewhat deviated from its asset-light model, TPL allowed itself to spend record amounts on acquisitions, but personally, I don’t find this a reason to worry. The company still has cash in its accounts and zero debt, and the growth of assets expands the royalty footprint and increases revenue without operating CAPEX. And the addition of water and SLEM (surface and limited easement and mineral) contracts reduces dependence on oil royalties since oil price volatility directly affects TPL’s account receipts. Which will ultimately increase the free cash flow needed to return capital, but through a short-term pause.

Toward Fair Value

I have already called the growth of quotes in 2024 a bubble, and I can repeat it again. Therefore, I consider the subsequent decline as a normalization of value. Both price movements had newsworthy reasons, but there was no fundamental basis.

At the time of the Q3 2024 report, the P/E GAAP was at 73x, so the current 47x no longer seems super expensive. Moreover, I find the premium valuation justified for such a business model and this segment. If we take into account the last 12 earnings releases, the median market valuation of the business is at ~42х, which is only 12% lower than the current price of $950 (as of July 24).

Using the historical multiples method, I can say that the price of $836 per share is a fair historical value, and I am planning to open a long-term position there.

Risks To The Thesis

Dependence on the oil and gas sector is the company's main vulnerability. A decrease in oil and gas prices, a reduction in drilling activity, or a change in the policy of large producers directly affect TPL's income. And we are talking not only about oil royalties but also about the water segment, which is directly related to the extraction of minerals. However, the geopolitical situation in the world in recent years has been a factor keeping energy prices from falling.

I also owned shares in a similar business and wrote an article Disappointed At Last After 3 Years Of Holding about Franco-Nevada (FNV), which suffered a significant crisis due to the closure of the largest mine in Panama. The TPL model theoretically assumes similar risks; however, they are significantly lower due to location and jurisdiction.

Bottom Line

I have done my best to thoroughly review all aspects of the company's operations and financial statements. A share price drop as steep as TPL's is always cause for concern and a search for objective reasons. However, in my opinion, we are simply seeing a return to normalcy and an opportunity to buy an outstanding business at a great price.

data.title: Avino Silver & Gold: Planning To Triple Production, Yet Still Flying Under-The-Radar
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Avino Silver & Gold Mines (ASM) is a silver miner with an excellent balance sheet that’s got very significant organic growth coming its way over the next couple of years, as one of its two mines (both located in Mexico) will become operational, as well as another high-potential project expected to become operational later this decade. Like a majority of silver miners, the company currently produces a mix of silver (49%), copper (31%), and gold (19%), as that is normal for silver deposits, although they see this returning to majority silver starting next year.

Following very impressive results lately, with silver equivalent production up 8%, gold output up 25% and copper up 19% - attributed primarily to improved grades and recoveries - on top of very significant jumps in the price in silver and gold this year, ASM stock is up a stellar 300% since the beginning of the year! This is actually one of the best YTD results in the entire gold/silver mining industry, although the company might still be flying under the radar of the general market due to its small size (around half a billion valuation after this 4x).

ASM expects significant growth in production thanks to their La Preciosa mine (a neighbor which they bought about 4 years ago) becoming fully operational, nearly doubling the company’s production by 2027 and potentially tripling it by 2029 thanks to the Oxide Tailings project where they’d reprocess older tailings.

Last year, with ~2.65 million silver equivalent ounces, the company managed to produce ~$17.9 million in free cash flows, reporting a record year in almost every number they reported. This comes at much lower commodity prices in 2024 compared to the current levels, as today we’re looking at 45-50% higher silver and gold prices.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that Avino is in an excellent position, with the current assets nearly covering twice the total liabilities, with nearly zero debt. This, together with no shareholder returns, as I’ll mention below, allows them to fully focus on the projects, with no external disruption. However, the equity stands at ~$132.2 million (similar to the market cap at the beginning of the year), while the current market cap reached $527 million. Still, remember that the balance sheet is backward-looking, and if you think about it, ~$108.2 million in plant, equipment, mining properties, exploration, and evaluation assets may seem pretty low in this environment, especially if we’re talking about a company that expects to produce a whopping 8 million silver equivalent ounces by 2029, potentially tripling its production compared to 2024.

As a note, the exploration and evaluation assets refer especially to Bralorne Gold Mines in Canada, which Avino bought back in 2014, and has held in a drilling/evaluation mode, and it’s not contributing to their production.

Shareholder Returns

Since Avino is focusing on developing its current projects, they don’t pay a dividend, nor do they do buybacks. In fact, the company has recently made a very wise move in my opinion by agreeing to potentially ISSUE $40 million worth of shares back in June. This is actually rare for the mining industry, as commodity producers generally do the opposite during the upside since they have the money for buybacks then, essentially wasting the money on buying shares at high levels instead of developing the business. In contrast, ASM is potentially issuing nearly 10% of the market cap, which would likely allow it to ensure a smooth and on-time finish for La Preciosa, but it could also open the way for more potential opportunities - perhaps buying “another” La Preciosa, which they bought for ~$20 million! Given their CAPEX of ~$6.6-8 million over the past years, $40 million definitely seems like something big is planned, but there is currently no details or plans for that, although I’d keep an eye out for that in their upcoming earnings report.

Risks & Opportunities

Silver is quite a fundamental and even future-facing commodity, almost 60% of its demand coming from industrial uses like electronics, solar panels, and alloys. Only about 15-20% goes into jewelry and investing/banks, respectively. What’s interesting is the way these sources of demand have evolved. While jewelry demand remained almost identical over the past decade, investment fell nearly 33%, but industrial uses went up significantly, thanks especially to electronics, which went up 78.3% during this time, and especially photovoltaics, up nearly 290% during this time, becoming the second highest source of demand for silver, surpassing investment and jewelry!

This is a very important development that probably came as a result of the production costs of photovoltaics falling by about 90% over the past decade. However, an important aspect to consider is that this could result in China accounting for an even higher amount of demand for silver, meaning that potential issues in China can result in issues for the demand, similar to copper, iron, and a majority of other commodities. In 2024, China’s industrial silver demand grew by a whopping 7%, and that’s before the country’s policy shift and economic measures meant to stimulate the economy were announced for 2025. This means that silver is much more tied to how the economy is doing, while gold's value is much more about the market’s sentiment. Back in April, market sentiment was almost at Extreme Fear levels based on the Fear and Green index.

Also, the low investment demand came at a time when gold went up significantly, while silver prices lagged it severely - until 2025, when it jumped 34% YTD. During bad/uncertain times for the economy (i.e., tariffs, potential trade war escalating, real wars, recessions, etc.), we might see a shift in this demand and a higher focus on silver from investment sources, similar to what happened to gold.

But, as I keep saying in other precious metals-related stock analyses, their stock price will also depend on the price of silver and gold first and foremost. During high prices, almost every miner on the planet is doing very well, backed by higher margins and more cash flows, leading to more investments, higher shareholder returns, etc. But, when prices fall during a downcycle, we’ll see silver miners having a tougher time, with potential debt issues, lower production, and even bankruptcies in some cases.

When it comes to gold, I believe this commodity has nearly unlimited potential, supported by its sources of demand. As I mentioned in previous articles, gold's demand sources are relatively unique compared even to other precious metals, allowing it to respond differently to economic factors compared to other commodities. Thanks to that, we can even see fear of missing out or the hype that I think is more and more visible nowadays, or other market phenomenon that can boost the price of gold even further since, unlike silver, gold doesn’t depend on “real life” rational uses - mostly jewelry, which has inelastic demand as it won’t go down significantly if gold jumps even more now, plus investing and banks. Especially if the market is then pushed into fear of a recession - or especially a real one - we can see that it sustains higher and higher prices for a longer time, which would be perfect for Avino.

I remain bullish on silver, gold, and copper (especially the latter), since we could see better prices thanks to several catalysts. I believe rate cuts are probably the main near-term catalyst to keep an eye out for when it comes to all of these commodities - and more - and I believe they are likely to come over the next couple of years, as Powell’s term ends in May 2026, and I think Trump is very likely to name someone who will obey him this time. Again, since he kept criticizing Powell for not cutting rates recently, I think we are likely to see him push his next proposed Fed chair into faster rate cuts, which could be amazing for commodity prices as it would weaken the dollar, which is great for precious metals (and also copper), while potentially making the market fear a harsh landing and even a recession (once again, great for precious metals). Also, I believe the change of the Fed’s chair and rate cuts potentially boosting silver and gold prices even more would probably come soon after the tariff situation becomes more clear (we recently saw hints of a potential US-EU deal, by the way), so the market would not have that much time to react to "calm" times and reevaluate its need for gold.

Another aspect that I want to mention about Avino flying under the radar is that the company doesn’t seem to be that popular when it comes to “big” money. 78% of the shares are held by retail investors/others, with the company’s management being a top shareholder, on top of various other financial institutions that each hold tiny stakes.

We’re talking about a very fragmented shareholder base, with the top 25 owners controlling ~15.86% of ASM. If we look at larger competitors, we see quite the opposite, with BlackRock, Van Eck, Vanguard, etc. owning significant shares, signaling that there is demand for their clients, which is not the case for ASM. I believe this is due to its overall tiny size and just not enough popularity, but if the stock continues to go up, it might attract the attention of the “right” money, which could then be followed by even more - being included in more capitalization-based funds and generating more attention/demand. In my opinion, this could be possible, especially if the company’s production develops as they plan, since it would then likely qualify as a bigger and more diversified miner.

As the company says, their 5-year expansion plan would basically turn it into an intermediate producer, representing the largest expansion in the company’s history! Still, this is not really a fundamentally-based catalyst, but rather one that depends mostly on the market’s sentiment, but it remains a possibility to keep in mind.

Another opportunity for Avino in particular could come from their potential as an acquisition target (on top of them buying someone after the $40 million issuing agreement) given the size and levels of production. Another company could scoop them out while the projects are almost fully operational and enjoy the boost in production. I think this could be possible even now after the 4x in stock price given the way commodities moved this year, especially since the company’s projects advanced significantly since the beginning of the year. We are talking about the difference from “maybe producing X” to “most likely producing X” (soon potentially confirmed in 2 quarters) - which can be worth a lot for a buyer that doesn’t want to take a high risk.

Still, a risk to keep in mind is their full exposure to Mexico. This means that any political or economic issues, for example Trump’s tariffs, could potentially affect the company even if they were not directly targeted. Weakening Mexico’s economy could lead to workforce/tax issues for Avino, while tariffs can lead to supply chain issues or other types of bottlenecks, especially if Mexico retaliates in any way (e.g. retaliatory tariffs would make mining equipment from the US more expensive).

Valuation

Starting with their $17.9 million in free cash flow last year assuming a 10% higher production but also 45% higher commodity prices, I’ll start the valuation with $28.55 million in free cash flow, assuming the same level of CAPEX. This FCF then grows at a CAGR that leads to a total increase of 185% by 2029 (meaning an outstanding exactly 30% - which I know is high, but it’s actually slightly below the company’s projections - although not as much if we assume 2024 as the starting year), bringing the 2029 FCF to approximately $81.56 million. After 2029, the FCF continues to grow at a 4.5% CAGR until 2034. The terminal growth rate is set at 2.5%. Using a WACC of 9%, and accounting for $21.6 million in net cash, with 145.21 million shares outstanding, the discounted cash flows and terminal value result in an enterprise value of approximately $1.10 billion. After adding the net cash, this leads to an equity value of about $1.12 billion, giving an intrinsic value of ~$7.73 per share, meaning a 117% increase from today’s level, despite the high jump YTD.

Of course, this assumes that silver and gold prices remain stable over such a long period, which is unlikely - although it can go either way - but I believe we should also keep in mind the likelihood of the company buying other mines if they develop so much, which could boost their growth pipeline significantly. The management’s recent history of execution remains somewhat mixed however, with La Preciosa being a great positive, while Bralorne Gold Mines is questionable to this date.

Conclusions

Overall, Avino is one of the most interesting silver mining companies in the market today. Despite the very significant jump in recent months, the company still has great potential if they reach their 2029 targets. From that point, they can focus on dividends, buybacks, more investments, etc., while the balance sheet remains exceptional. So, I rate ASM a Buy now, backed by their financial health and massive potential to grow in the following years.

data.title: Too Big To Win: Why America's Favorite Stocks Could Ruin Your Retirement
data.description:
Introduction

While this may be one of the most useless sentences I have ever written in an article, I sometimes wish I were five years older than I'm now.

It's nonsense for many reasons, including the fact that every single change in our lives impacts our entire future. This includes the butterfly effect, which is a concept within chaos theory.

Essentially, it makes the case that a very small change in one part of a deterministic nonlinear system can result in large differences in a later state.

For example, if we go back to the first theories of how COVID-19 started (I know it's more complex, but this is just an example), we can assume that a Chinese man eating a bat in Wuhan resulted in central banks pumping trillions into the market, causing inflation to rise and home prices to explode.

Or what if your parents had made just one different decision in their lives? They may have never met. You would be a completely different person today.

Anyway, I think you get the point.

The reason I'm bringing all of this up is that I started investing in 2011. At that time, I was 15 or 16 years old. As I've written in previous articles, my approach was less about genuine investing and more about buying leveraged products and random stocks, pretending I knew what I was doing.

It took until 2020 for me to have both substantial knowledge of the market and the funds to start a serious portfolio. Before that, I had decent knowledge but not enough money to start any meaningful wealth journey.

A big part of our investment journey depends on luck, which is something Warren Buffett has brought up as well:

"The luckiest day in my life is the day I was born, you know, 'cause I was born in the United States."

"I was just lucky... We've gone through all kinds of things ... If I were being born today, you know, I would just keep negotiating in the womb until (they) said you can be in the United States. We're all pretty lucky." - Warren Buffett (via Reuters)

While our roots are important, my point is the importance of timing. Assuming all else is equal, our market entries are crucial and something we cannot always impact.

For example, investors who entered the market years. Including inflation, it's much longer, as higher consumer prices pressure our real worth.

The same happened to investors who entered the market in the early 2000s, as they went through the Dot-com bust and the Great Financial Crisis, all within the span of roughly 12 years before the market was breakeven again.

This, too, excludes inflation.

Then there are investors who entered the market in the early 1980s or after the Great Financial Crisis. Their timing could not have been more favorable. While both periods saw substantial sell-offs, including 1987 and the 2020 pandemic, overall returns were much higher than the market's average.

While I am quite happy that I started to get serious about dividend (growth) investing in June of 2020 (before that, I mainly traded), I often think about what would have happened to my net worth if I had started just five years earlier.

On an "all-else-equal" basis, I probably would be sitting on much more capital right now, as the past ten years have been truly spectacular.

That's also why I am writing this article, as I truly believe anyone working on a retirement plan may be in for a big surprise.

Although it may not seem that way, underlying fundamentals are an increasing issue, which could derail some of our plans and "force" us to make adjustments.

So, as we have a lot more to discuss, let's get to the next part!

Winners Win

We're not that far away from football season, which is great news for anyone who enjoys watching college and NFL games. I'm one of these people.

The interesting thing about football is that sometimes, only a few very talented players can make a huge impact. This includes the "Legion of Boom" in Seattle, consisting of Richard Sherman, Earl Thomas, and Kam Chancellor in the 2010s; the "Triplets" of Troy Aikman, Emmitt Smith, and Michael Irvin in Dallas during the 1990s; and the duo of Tom Brady and Rob Gronkowski in New England in the 2010s, among others, of course.

As a fan, it doesn't matter how a team wins games, as long as it's consistently winning and making it far in the playoffs, ideally winning the championship.

The stock market is similar.

  • The biggest ten stocks of the index account for nearly 40% of its weighting. That's one of the highest numbers in its history.

  • The ratio of the market value of the biggest five stocks to all small stocks combined is nearly 5.0x, a mindblowing number if you consider that we're talking about just five stocks.

  • Semiconductors (that's an industry, not a sector) account for 11% of the entire S&P 500. Nvidia (NVDA) alone accounts for nearly 8% of it.

Looking a bit closer (using the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO)), we see that Nvidia and Microsoft (MSFT) combined account for more than 14% of the market. When adding Apple (AAPL), that number is almost 20%.

To use the words of Lawrence McDonald:

There are few things in this world more foolish than placing 8% of America's entire 401k system in a $4T stock at 27x sales.

That said, I'm not a "hater."

These companies are among the most innovative on the planet, with an impressive ability to scale their operations. They are like the few players who boost an entire team, with Nvidia being the Tom Brady of stocks.

Since 2021 (I wish the data would go back further), the Magnificent-7 stocks have accounted for 33 to 63% of the market's entire returns. Without these stocks, the S&P 500's return would have been far from spectacular.

Since 2015, the S&P 500 has "crushed" its equal-weighted peer ETF (RSP), as the ratio between SPY/RSP below shows. Before that, the market's performance was much more balanced.

But then again, who cares? Right?

If your favorite team wins every championship over the next ten years just because of a few really good players, it won't matter much. A win is a win.

The problem is that this creates huge risks.

Watch Out When The Music Stops

Just like in this article, I have often made the case that America's biggest companies are great businesses. That almost goes without saying, as we have all seen the evidence.

However, I have also started to make the case that this isn't 2019 anymore. We have gone past cloud computing and fast-selling iPhones.

Now, AI is the dominating story.

And, unlike cloud computing and smartphones, AI has the power to disrupt the economy as we know it, as cheesy as that may sound.

For example, AI is rapidly becoming a very efficient employee, as some AI insiders make the case that unemployment among white-collar employees could reach 20% over the next five years.

On top of that, it's able to create life-like videos and photos, which could disrupt the entire entertainment industry over the next few years.

The other day, I was with people who told me they had found a great video about homes in Austria. They were homes built in the mountains with spectacular views. The only problem is that I quickly found out they had been watching an AI-generated video for the better part of an hour.

As analysts believe the biggest four AI hyperscalers will spend close to $330 billion in annual CapEx on AI (see below), I think it's fair to say they are very serious about these opportunities.

Personally, I'm worried about the future, and not because I have no idea if AI will ever be able to learn to write articles and do research like me.

It also has major implications for the stock market, as the biggest companies are now all fighting for the top spot in the AI world, something that could punish the companies who fail to innovate. That's why I am so bullish on companies further down the supply chain, like I wrote in this article.

The Wall Street Journal covered this as well, as it noted how AI is creating huge differences between these Big Tech players:

Apple, for instance, has failed to woo investors with its AI efforts. Last year, it introduced its Apple Intelligence service to great fanfare, only to come up short on its promises. The company has said it would share updates about its AI-powered Siri in the coming year, meaning it might not reach the market until late 2026.

Morgan, of Synovus, said he recently trimmed his Apple holdings for the first time in years. When a client was looking to make a donation, he recommended gifting Apple equities, something he wouldn't have done years ago. Other investors have reduced Apple positions in favor of Nvidia or Microsoft. - WSJ

Essentially, I fully agree with the Journal's takeaway, as it notes that the FANG acronym may be something of the past, as the dynamics have changed.

The previous era was defined by FAANG-Facebook's parent, Meta; Amazon; Apple; Netflix; and Google's owner, Alphabet-until that group fizzled in 2023. If these companies' stocks continue heading in wildly different directions, that might leave the door open for a newly named group of hot stocks-and the Magnificent Seven could go the way of FAANG or the Nifty Fifty. - WSJ

We also see a strong convergence in growth rates between the Magnificent-7 and the S&P 493, which begs the question of whether paying a premium is still worth it.

Obviously, the party can continue for a while.

However, if history is any suggestion, it's usually that these periods end in subdued longer-term returns. Below, I bring up three important periods:

  • Dot-Com bubble (1999-2010): Tech stocks dominated, with the top 10 having a weighting of roughly 27% of the S&P 500 in 2000. With a CAPE ratio of 44, the market crashed 49% (2000-2002). Even worse, it delivered 0% nominal and negative real returns over a decade.
  • Nifty fifty/stagflation (1965-1982): The Nifty Fifty stocks led the S&P 500, with P/E ratios of more than 50 and a CAPE of 24 in 1965. Stagflation drove 5.8% nominal and 0% real annualized returns, with real recovery to the 1968 peak by 1982.
  • Great depression (1929-1940s): Industrial and utility stocks dominated the Dow, with a CAPE of 32 in 1929. The market fell 89% by 1932, which was followed by decades of poor real returns due to economic turmoil.

This is a big deal.

As you can imagine, the S&P 500 is a big driver of retirement wealth.

While I expect that many retirees have shifted their capital to higher-yielding investments, the S&P 500 is still a major part of many accounts of retirees and most people looking to retire in the future.

According to Goldman Sachs, U.S. households held $8.9 trillion in equities through retirement accounts like 401(k)s in 2024, with an average of 90% of 401(k) allocations for participants in their 20s directed toward stocks, often via S&P 500 index funds.

Moreover, roughly 60% of Americans have a retirement savings plan, with 89% of those with such plans also holding stock market investments, primarily through S&P 500-linked investment products.

In other words, it's very bad news that we are dealing with a mix of:

  • A very top-heavy market.
  • A valuation that implies a subdued longer-term total return.

Don't get me wrong. This is by no means an attempt to get anyone to sell their shares. Every family member of mine who we have gotten into S&P 500 index funds isn't selling a single share.

However, we are making changes.

A Shift We Can Benefit From

Generally speaking, I believe in gradual adjustments for improved long-term returns. As I wrote in a recent article titled "Attention Dividend Investors: The Macro Shift You've Been Waiting For Is Here," the goal is to find undervalued equities with strong balance sheets, inflation protection, and stellar business models to generate long-term value.

This includes dividend (growth) stocks, which have been left behind. In this case, I am talking about the kind of stocks owned by ETFs like Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM). Both have underperformed the market since the tech boom started. SCHD, for example, holds some of the market's most reliable dividend stocks with a weighted average yield of 4% and high-single-digit long-term annual dividend growth.

Especially if we consider that the market's earnings yield (inverted P/E ratio) is now below the 10-year government bond's yield, it makes sense to look for income and value in other areas.

In fact, I even believe that a simple investment in the equal-weighted S&P 500 could bear fruit for the next ten years.

Essentially, I'm focusing on companies that aren't hogging the spotlight but have the kind of boring, reliable fundamentals that keep delivering through economic storms.

Think consumer staples, utilities, or even industrials with consistent cash flows and reasonable valuations. In general, I am increasingly bullish on cyclical value, including railroads like Union Pacific (UNP), a company I expect to beat the market for many years to come.

To go back to my football reference, these are the players that might not win the Super Bowl every year, but they're making the playoffs on a very consistent basis. They are also less prone to injury, meaning if the best stocks fail to carry the market, these stocks should be able to deliver alpha.

Moreover, I think we should focus on companies that benefit from AI in ways other than the Big Tech giants. This includes my investments in LandBridge (LB), Texas Pacific Land (TPL), and Comfort Systems USA (FIX). I recently covered LB and TPL in this article.

Generally speaking, I expect AI benefits to trickle down, shifting the "AI trade" from Big Tech to companies who are able to successfully implement AI, potentially creating substantially higher margins.

Needless to say, because picking the right investments is so complex and because there are countless good ideas, I will spend a lot more time on finding the right investments in the weeks, months, and years ahead.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

Sometimes, I wish I had started investing five years earlier, just to catch a bigger part of the bull market that still felt like easy money.

But timing isn't something we control, and luck plays a bigger role than we admit, whether it's when we're born or when we enter the market.

It's just something we have to deal with.

And, to be honest, that's fine. Without it, life would be boring.

Right now, the S&P 500 is a superstar team carried by a handful of players that include Nvidia, Microsoft, and Apple.

While a win is a win, concentration brings risk, which is what this entire article was based on.

History shows these dominance cycles don't end well for long-term returns.

So, I'm shifting.

I'm not selling, but diversifying into undervalued dividend growers, cyclicals like railroads, and AI beneficiaries beyond Big Tech with the goal to play defense without missing the next rally.

I think it's a low-risk way of potentially generating substantial alpha for many years to come.

At least, that's the goal.

Let me know what you think!

data.title: YETI Holdings: Sell This Stock As U.S. Sales Falter And Tariffs Eat Into FCF (Downgrade)
data.description:
To me, a skyrocketing stock market continues to diverge drastically from the underlying weakness in the macroeconomy. Earnings results from consumer-facing companies alone help to attest to a slowdown in spending and confidence, and investors should take care to capitalize on recent market strength to lock in gains and wait for a much bigger dip before buying.

YETI Holdings (NYSE:YETI), in my view, is a company that is increasingly vulnerable. The outdoor products company has sharply cut its outlook for the year, making its valuation proposition shaky at best. It's pinning its hopes on international growth: but at the same time, overall margins are falling, hurt in no small part by tariffs. In spite of these headwinds, general market optimism has helped YETI to recover basically all of the losses it incurred after tariffs were announced, gaining ~30% from April lows in the mid-$20s. The question for investors now is: is YETI positioned for a rally, or is this a dead cat bounce?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on YETI in February, when the stock was trading near $38. Since then, YETI has fallen sharply on tariff news and then rebounded again: but at the same time, the company's pace of growth has slowed severely, coupled with meaningful losses in gross margin and overall profitability. Also notable is the fact that YETI has reduced its free cash flow guidance for FY25 by nearly half. With macroeconomic headwinds pressuring this company severely, I don't think YETI's rebound sufficiently prices in the added risks that the company faces, and I'm downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

With the guidance cut, YETI no longer looks cheap

Let's start with the number-one reason I'm pivoting to a bearish sentiment on YETI. Previously, the stock held some appeal as a value play, albeit one with a choppy track record for execution and slowing sales since the pandemic (especially after its rival Stanley took off in popularity).

That appeal has vanished, however, with YETI's deep guidance cut. The company is now only expecting 1-4% y/y adjusted sales growth this year (adjusted due to a 53-week fiscal year in FY25, versus a standard 52-week calendar in FY24) - indicating less than half of the company's previous growth rate expectations at 5-7% y/y. Making matters worse, when adjusting for its latest view on tariffs, YETI has also dropped its operating margin outlook to 12.0%, 490bps worse versus its prior outlook.

As a result, YETI's adjusted EPS guidance was also cut by -32% at the midpoint to $1.99, while FCF is now expected at $100-$125 million, or a -43% cut at the midpoint versus a prior $200 million expectation.

At current share prices near $37, YETI trades at a market cap of $3.05 billion. After we net off the $259.0 million of cash and $77.9 million of debt on YETI's latest balance sheet (or a $181 million net cash position that's worth ~6% of its current market value), the company's resulting enterprise value is $2.87 billion.

This positions YETI's valuation multiples at:

  • 18.5x FY25 P/E
  • 17.4x FY25 ex-cash P/E
  • 25.3x EV/FY25 FCF

For a company that is barely growing (and indeed, actually declining in the U.S.), these are not multiples I'm comfortable paying. The issue is that I don't view any of the current headwinds for YETI to be transitory in nature: and as such, I doubt that YETI shares will have the capacity to rebound meaningfully higher.

With U.S. sales falling, YETI's international sales are critical: but can growth last?

In YETI's latest quarter, sales in the U.S. declined -2% y/y to $271.3 million, or about 77% of the company's overall revenue. The only reason YETI was able to generate low single-digit revenue growth was via sharp growth in international, where revenue grew an impressive 22% y/y to $79.9 million.

International expansion is the core "upside risk" I see to my bear thesis on YETI, but the sustainability of this growth is shaky, in my view. YETI bulls will point to the fact that YETI has a relatively small share of overseas sales relative to other brands. The chart below showcases brands like Nike (NKE), with 57% of sales made overseas, alongside Deckers Outdoor (DECK) and Lululemon (LULU) in the mid/high 30s.

This sales profile suggests that, relative to YETI's current ~20% international revenue mix, the company could have the potential to double (or even greater) its current international revenue scale. As shown above, the company is currently only live in a few Western European countries, Canada, and the UK, and is planning on a broader-scale launch in Japan this year.

Here's what I view as the issue, however. Nike, Lululemon, Deckers, Under Armour (UA), and the other companies listed above are more generalized apparel makers. YETI, meanwhile, has a more specific outdoorsy-based product portfolio. In fact, one of its core signature products, the ice cooler, specifically gained popularity driven by the American football tailgate culture.

Yes, YETI also has general-appeal products like tumblers and camping cookware: certainly, the company can benefit from enthusiastic outdoorsmanship in European and Canadian markets. But as macro headwinds weigh on global consumer spending, and as trade tensions rise (leading to consumers in countries like Canada boycotting U.S. products), I have doubts that YETI will be able to sustain >20% growth internationally for a long period of time.

Tariffs will be a long-term drag on YETI's margins

The other headwind that deserves mention, beyond dragging U.S. sales, is YETI's exposure to tariffs. Over the past few years, YETI has indeed done a good job of diversifying its manufacturing supply chain outside of China. The company notes that on a go-forward basis, less than 5% of its total COGS will be exposed to U.S. tariffs on China.

That doesn't mean, however, that YETI has a get-out-of-jail-free card for its tariff bill. As shown in the global map above, YETI still has a large concentration of its supply chain in Asia: particularly in the Philippines, Indonesia (where President Trump recently announced trade deals amounting to a 19% headline tariff for both nations), and Vietnam, where the tariff rate is 20% (or 40% for goods that are deemed to be transshipments from China).

It's important to note that YETI's current outlook assumes only a 10% duty rate on non-Chinese nations (whereas the recent negotiations peg the major Southeast Asian nations at a ~20% tariff rate). Per CFO Mike McMullen's remarks on the Q1 earnings call in mid-May:

We now expect gross margins of approximately 54%, which is a decline of approximately 450 basis points versus both the prior year and our original FY2025 expectations. The primary driver of the decline is the impact of tariffs.

In this outlook, we are assuming that the following tariffs on products that we import into the U.S. are in place through the end of the year, a 145% total tariff rate on products sourced from China and a 10% reciprocal tariff rate on products sourced from all other countries where applicable to our business. Note that we are assuming that the current pause on higher reciprocal tariff rates on products sourced from non-China countries is extended to the end of 2025. Collectively, the gross amount of tariffs that are included in this outlook is approximately $100 million. That is before our mitigation efforts, which include pricing as well as cost offsets in partnership with our suppliers. Net of our mitigation efforts, the impact from tariffs that is included in this outlook is approximately 450 basis points."

Given the jump from 10% reciprocal tariff rates to ~20% in Southeast Asia, we expect YETI to further drop its outlook when it next releases earnings for Q2.

Key takeaways

To me, YETI faces a long list of risks: sluggish U.S. sales growth, the potential for deceleration in international sales growth, sharp tariff impacts that are eating into the company's margin profile and FCF, and a valuation that doesn't exactly leave much room for upside.

In my view, YETI's recent rebound rally is a great opportunity for investors to shed this stock and protect their capital: we'll have another chance to buy YETI down the road for a much cheaper price once updated tariff risks are fully priced in.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Data Center And LNG Optimism Builds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Baker Hughes Overview

Shares of Baker Hughes (BKR) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 31%. These gains intensified Wednesday, with Baker adding about 10% thanks to extremely strong quarterly results and commentary. Baker Hughes continues to be a major beneficiary of increased US LNG activity, and it now is seeing increased benefits from data centers. I upgraded shares in June to “buy,” and since then, the stock has added 16%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BKR.

Cost Controls Support Baker Hughes' Q2 Results

In the company’s second quarter, Baker Hughes earned $0.63, which beat estimates by $0.08. While revenue was down 2.7% from last year at $6.9 billion, this was $300 million better than feared. Baker Hughes operates across two business lines: its legacy oil field services business and its growth unit focused on LNG and industrial power. Weak oil prices have weighed on its legacy business, while secular growth is powering new opportunities.

The combination of the two left BKR with modestly lower revenue than last year, but the company is generating significant operating efficiencies. Adjusted EBITDA grew by 7% to $1.2 billion as margins expanded by 170 bps from last year to 17.5% thanks to cost cuts. Indeed, BKR is generating efficiencies across segments. As a result, the company produced $239 million of free cash flow in the quarter.

IET Orders Remain Brisk With Added AI Upside

Looking into segment results, Industrial & Energy Technology (“IET”) revenue grew by 5% to $3.3 billion, and EBITDA margins of 17.8% expanded by 190 bps from last year. As a result, EBITDA grew by 18% to $585 million. This unit is the primary source of enthusiasm in shares and is what can make Baker Hughes a growth stock. There is tremendous investment along the Gulf Coast to increase our LNG export capacity, and given the large scale of these projects, this provides multiple years of revenue.

Indeed, as I have written about in the past, the primary challenge for Baker Hughes is scaling up its capacity to meet demand. It is supply, and not customer demand, that is capping how much revenue can grow. During the quarter, there were IET orders of $3.5 billion, up 2% from last year. This segment had a book to bill ratio of about 1.08x.

IET now has a backlog of $31.3 billion, providing over 2 years of future revenue. Especially with orders continuing to be higher than revenue, this provides a clear and predictable source of growth for the company. Importantly, while LNG-related activity remains the core source of the business, Baker Hughes is now seeing benefits from the AI-related data center booms, given these facilities need power and cooling capacity.

During the quarter, $550 million in power generation equipment orders from data centers. It aimed to achieve $1.7 billion of data center orders by the end of 2027, but it should exceed this. Baker Hughes now has agreements to supply 30 turbines powering data centers across the US. Again, these are multiyear projects, and importantly, initial equipment sales provide future, recurring service revenue, which tends to be higher margin. This creates a favorable cycle of recurring and growing revenue as its installed base increases. I would not view BKR as primarily an AI-infrastructure play. Rather, this boom just provides an additional lever for accelerating IET growth.

Cost Management In OFSE Impresses

The other side of the business is facing more challenges. Oilfield Services & Equipment (“OFSE”) revenue was $3.6 billion. This was up 3% from Q1, consistent with seasonal patterns. Impressively, despite the decline in sales, margins were 18.7%, leading to $677 million of EBITDA. Even with 10% lower revenue, margins expanded by 90 bps. We are clearly in an oil & gas downcycle, but BKR is managing through it extremely well.

Within OFSE, well construction revenue fell by 16% to $921 million, with completions also down by 16%. North America was down 9%, while Latin America was down just 4%. The Middle East is its most important region at $1.4 billion of revenue, and this was down 7%. With OPEC+ now increasing production, the Middle East should be nearing a bottom. Still, with more supply coming online and weak global demand growth, I struggle to see a surge in oil prices and expect OFSE activity to remain muted.

BKR Has A Strong Balance Sheet And Credible Guidance

Critically, Baker Hughes has an absolutely pristine balance sheet. It has $3.1 billion of cash and just $6 billion of debt, resulting in only 0.6x net debt to EBITDA leverage. Plus, portfolio optimization actions that I discussed in June should generate about $1 billion in cash. As a result, BKR has ample capacity to continue and even increase the pace of its capital returns. BKR did $196 million of buybacks in Q2, and its share count is down 1% from last year. BKR also has a 2% dividend yield, which I expect to grow annually.

Alongside results, management largely retained companywide guidance, but there were several shifts within guidance that point to the acceleration we are seeing in IET against the ongoing weakness of OFSE. It expects IET EBITDA to be $50 million better at $2.35 billion, but it maintained total company EBITDA of $4.675 billion, given pressure in its legacy OFSE business.

Management reiterated its view upstream oil & gas spending will decline high-single digits with North American activity underperforming. Management continues to expect full year IET orders of $12.5-$14.5 billion. Guidance also reflects the fact that Q2 had a $15 million tariff impact, and it sees $100 million of tariff costs in H2.

Given the orders in Q2 as well as acceleration in data center demand, I expect orders to come in the top-half of its IET guidance. Back in June, I saw the company delivering at the high end of its IET guidance while OFSE would decline by 10-13%. Management’s shifts in guidance essentially reflect my view that IET is strengthening and OFSE will be weaker for longer.

One upside risk over the medium term would be if President Trump can indeed get Asian partners to help finance a mega-LNG project in Alaska. Existing orders already support strong IET growth, but this project could provide meaningful further growth to BKR starting in 2027; I see no material impact before then. My view is that this project does not occur, but it could ultimately be a multi-billion opportunity for the company.

After The Rally, BKR Stock Is At Fair Value

Back in June, I was targeting about $2.40 +/- $.05 in EPS. This drove a fair value estimate of $42, which shares have now eclipsed. While I view the revenue picture as unchanged, BKR’s cost management in OFSE continues to exceed my own expectations, and so I now see EPS of $2.42-$2.52. I have viewed a ~17x multiple as appropriate, reflecting growth in IET and the slower-growth trajectory of OFSE.

Now, given the market’s focus on AI and some incremental lift from data centers, it is possible that BKR can sustain a higher multiple. Still, this is not a pure-play AI company by any means, the majority of revenue still comes from OFSE where no major revenue recovery is imminent. I would struggle to pay more than 18x, even reflecting data center upside, pointing to about $45-$46.

With shares having rallied 16% since my last article, I believe that the buy thesis has played out, occurring more quickly than I expected. With shares around fair value, I no longer view BKR as a “buy.” As such, I am downgrading shares to a “hold.” I do not see a rush to sell as BKR has an excellent balance sheet, is not overvalued, and has exposure to two key growth sectors: LNG & data centers. If we saw shares rally past $48, I would sell, and similarly, I would be a buyer if shares pull back to $40. At their current level, I expect BKR to be a market-performer going forward.

data.title: AT&T: Solid Second Quarter, More Upside Likely Long Term
data.description:
Introduction

Despite the challenging macro environment from higher for longer interest rates and now tariffs, telecom companies have performed well in the past year. AT&T (NYSE:T), once a Dividend Aristocrat, has been making headway after cutting their dividend a few years ago.

Their latest earnings report was solid, showing progress despite fiber subscribers missing the consensus, which resulted in a brief sell-off. But if the company can continue on its path, I think there's more upside to be had over the next year or two.

In this article, I discuss AT&T's latest performance, fundamentals, and why investors could see further upside, even after their strong price rally in the past year.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered AT&T back in February in an article titled: Strong Price Performance Justified With Potential For Further Upside.

Since, the stock has continued its rally, up over 12% compared to the S&P's (SP500) nearly 14.5% in the past 5 months.

AT&T's strong rally was justified by their improved efficiencies, debt reduction, and announced buyback program.

As a result, I rated the stock a buy, as they also offered significant upside to their price target of nearly $34 if the stock could return to its historical valuation.

Solid Quarter

AT&T reported their Q2 earnings and managed to beat on both their top and bottom lines despite fewer fiber subscribers than the Bloomberg consensus. EPS of $0.54 beat by a penny, while their top line beat by a solid $370 million.

Revenue amounted to $30.8 billion. As a result, both their top and bottom lines grew 3.4% and 5.9%, respectively, from the previous year. For comparison, peer Verizon (VZ) saw slightly higher growth in both metrics year-over-year.

VZ's revenue increased 5.2% from $32.8 billion to $34.5 billion, while earnings per share increased 6.1% from $1.15 to $1.19.

AT&T's performance was driven by higher net income and higher mobility & consumer wireline revenues. Net income increased from $3.9 billion to $4.9 billion. Postpaid phone net adds were 401,000, while the aforementioned fiber subscriber net additions amounted to 243,000, falling below the 252,000 consensus.

Consumer fiber broadband revenues grew an impressive 18.9% to $2.1 billion from the prior year. The company also divested their remaining 70% stake in DirecTV during the quarter.

Growing Cash Flows

When it comes to investing in telecom companies, growing cash flows and deleveraging are important metrics investors like to see. During the quarter, T's free cash flow increased from $4 billion to $4.4 billion, a result of higher cash from operations, which was up $700 million to $9.8 billion.

Moreover, T expects to recognize cash savings between $1.5 billion and $2 billion this year. In the next two years, they expect this to increase to $2.5 billion to $3 billion.

As far as free cash flow growth, management is anticipating this is a range of $16+ billion this year, a decline from the previous year's $17.6 billion. But in 2026 and beyond, T is expecting to get back on the path to free cash flow growth with $18+ billion in 2026 and $19+ billion in 2027.

If they can achieve this, this would give them more flexibility for continuous share repurchases. Furthermore, this would likely drive earnings growth, leading to upside potential over the long term.

Balance Sheet

The company also continued to make a dent in its balance sheet. Net debt stood at $120.3 billion, while total debt was $132.3 billion. For comparison, Verizon's net debt was slightly lower at $116 billion and total debt of $146 billion.

T's debt declined from the previous year's quarter's $126.9 billion and $130.6 billion, respectively. Verizon's decreased from $122.8 billion and $149.3 billion, respectively. T's leverage stood at 2.63x compared to VZ's 2.3x.

Cash & equivalents amounted to $10.5 billion. Continued debt reduction and share repurchases will also likely drive further price appreciation. So far this year, management has bought back $1.3 billion shares and $1 billion worth in Q2 alone. For 2025, they plan to repurchase $4 billion worth.

Well-Covered Dividend

As previously mentioned, AT&T's free cash flow amounted to $4.4 billion during the quarter and $7.5 billion for the first half, with $4.1 billion in dividends paid.

As a result, AT&T's free cash flow payout ratio sits at 54.6%. This was notably lower than Verizon's 64.7%, as their free cash flow for the first half of 2025 amounted to $8.8 billion, with $5.7 billion paid out in the form of dividends.

This gives T ample room for further buyback implementations and dividend increases. Additionally, there's a good chance shareholders could see a dividend increase in the near future, which could also increase investors' confidence and drive price appreciation since the dividend has remained flat since 2022.

2025 Outlook

Although they didn't raise their guidance like Verizon, T's outlook for the year remains solid with strong Consumer Fiber Broadband revenue growth of double-digits. EPS, like free cash flow, is expected to decline from $2.26 the previous year. But beyond 2025, T looks to accelerate growth, which could lead to further upside in the next year or two.

More Upside Likely In The Next 12 To 24 Months

Despite negative growth this year, T looks poised for solid growth ahead with an expected growth rate of 5.7% over the next 5 years. If so, I expect their share price to follow, especially since interest rates will likely be lower by then.

Using the Discounted Cash Flow Method, T's upside from the current price of $27.47 is appealing with a price target of $36 a share. I use a 4% growth rate afterwards to account for their mature business and an 11% expected return, slightly above the S&P's historical 7%–10% due to their higher yield and lower growth.

Risks & Conclusion

One risk that could impact AT&T is the FED's decision regarding interest rates. While many still expect two rate cuts this year, tariffs could impact this, leading to a potential sell-off in the sector as a result of their high debt loads.

Investors should watch this closely and look to add on any share price volatility in the coming months. Moreover, the upcoming August 1st deadline could also result in a sell-off, although this could change at any time.

If further tariffs are implemented and these prove to be inflationary, T's segments could see further weakening as consumers remain financially constrained as the FED will likely continue to keep rates elevated.

But if you're a long-term investor, these should be viewed as opportunities, as I view T's low forward multiple of 13.59x as attractive.

As a result of this, their expected cost savings, free cash flow growth, and share buybacks, I think AT&T is poised to see further upside in the next 1 to 2 years, making them a buy at the current price.
